skill.name: error-logger
skill.description: Structured JSON logging with correlation IDs for multi-service systems. Use when implementing logging, debugging failures, or tracing errors across services.
skill.summary: Guide AI assistants to add structured error logging with correlation IDs.
skill.author: BarisSozen

security_audit.summary: Static findings were reviewed against the single skill file. The external command, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance detections are false positives from Markdown examples and descriptive text, with no executable code or prompt injection found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Examples Flagged as External Commands
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick detections occur in Markdown code fences and inline formatting. The file contains JSON and TypeScript examples only, with no executable shell command.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are inside Markdown documentation and contain log format examples. No Ruby backtick execution or shell command invocation is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Detection
low_findings.description: The flagged description line references correlation IDs and error tracing, not a weak cryptographic algorithm. No hashing, encryption, or random generation implementation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is frontmatter text describing logging behavior. There is no cryptographic API or algorithm selection to evaluate.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Detection
low_findings.description: The flagged line shows propagation of an X-Correlation-ID HTTP header. It does not gather host, user, network, or system information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is an HTTP header example for tracing requests. No reconnaissance command, API, or data collection behavior appears in the file.

content.value_statement: Distributed services are difficult to debug when errors lack shared context. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to add JSON logs with correlation IDs.
actual_capabilities: Defines a structured JSON log format for error events.
Recommends correlation IDs for related log entries.
Shows how to propagate correlation IDs through HTTP headers.
Provides log level guidance for errors, warnings, info, and debug data.
Suggests operation prefixes for different service workflows.
limitations: Does not include a logging library implementation.
Does not enforce schemas at runtime.
Does not configure log storage, alerts, or dashboards.
Does not define privacy rules for sensitive log fields.

use_cases.title: Add tracing to service failures
use_cases.description: Use the skill to add correlation IDs and consistent error fields across related service calls.

use_cases.title: Standardize operational logs
use_cases.description: Use the skill to align log levels, event names, and contextual fields across a codebase.

use_cases.title: Improve incident debugging
use_cases.description: Use the skill to make production failures easier to follow during incident review.

prompt_templates.title: Add basic error logs
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for one function or endpoint.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the error-logger skill to add structured error logging to this function. Include a correlation ID and clear event type.

prompt_templates.title: Trace a request path
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request across multiple service calls.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the error-logger skill to trace this request flow. Propagate the correlation ID through each service boundary.

prompt_templates.title: Review logging consistency
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the error-logger skill to review these logging patterns. Identify missing fields, unclear event types, and inconsistent log levels.

prompt_templates.title: Design an error logging plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced planning for a multi-service system.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the error-logger skill to design a structured logging plan for this architecture. Include correlation ID rules, event naming, and level guidance.

output_examples.input: Add error logging to a payment failure handler.
output_examples.output: The assistant adds an error log with timestamp, level, correlation ID, service name, event type, message, and contextual payment fields.

output_examples.input: Trace an error across an API gateway and worker.
output_examples.output: The assistant keeps the same correlation ID across both services and explains where to pass it in the request headers.
The assistant reports missing correlation IDs, unclear event names, and log levels that do not match the documented guidance.

best_practices: Create one correlation ID at the start of each operation.
Use stable event type names that can be searched reliably.
Keep sensitive user data out of log context fields.
anti_patterns: Do not create a new correlation ID for every log line in one operation.
Do not place secrets, tokens, or private data in logs.
Do not use DEBUG logs for required production incident data.

faq.question: Does this skill install a logging package?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance for structured logging patterns and examples.

faq.question: Can it work with any programming language?
faq.answer: Yes, the concepts are language neutral. The included usage example is TypeScript-style documentation.

faq.question: What is a correlation ID?
faq.answer: It is an identifier shared by related logs so one operation can be traced across services.

faq.question: Does it send logs to an external service?
faq.answer: No. The skill does not include network code or external log shipping instructions.

faq.question: Can it help with incident response?
faq.answer: Yes. Consistent event types and correlation IDs make production failures easier to investigate.

faq.question: Should sensitive data be logged?
faq.answer: No. Secrets, tokens, personal data, and private payloads should be removed or redacted.
